title: Power, dilution and the AI chokepoint
gist: The 12 June abrupt global disablement of Anthropic's Mythos-class models — taken offline for everyone after a White House directive to restrict access to all foreign nationals — showed how readily one government can cut off global AI access, with neither the alliance nor Five Eyes protecting Australia. The author argues most countries cannot win the AGI race, so the strategic goal is 'power dilution' via exploiting physical chokepoints (minerals, energy, compute) and collective AI governance to prevent a winner-takes-all AGI hegemon.

themes.theme: The Mythos shutoff as proof of concentration

insights.claim: On 12 June global access to Anthropic's new Mythos-class models — described as the most capable ever released — was abruptly disabled after a White House directive to restrict access to all foreign nationals, and Anthropic took the models offline for everyone because it could not verify user nationality in real time.
insights.speaker: Tom Barber
insights.so_what: This was the first use of export controls to restrict access to AI models (not chips/weights); model-access continuity is now a state-level political risk for any AI-dependent business outside the US.
insights.quote: Anthropic took the models offline for everyone
insights.locator: On 12 June, global access to Anthropic's new Mythos-class models

insights.claim: The shutoff came days after Trump signed an executive order requesting early government access to every frontier model before public release, showing an administration explicit about winning the AI race exercising state-level power concentration.
insights.speaker: Tom Barber
insights.so_what: US government pre-release access to frontier models signals deepening state capture of AI labs; a structural risk premium for non-US reliance on US frontier AI.
insights.locator: And it came only days after Trump signed

insights.claim: Australia's AI access disappeared alongside everyone else's, and neither the alliance nor Five Eyes membership made any difference.
insights.speaker: Tom Barber
insights.so_what: Alliance status confers no protection on AI access; allied nations should price in being cut off and build sovereign/diluted alternatives.
insights.quote: neither the alliance nor membership of Five Eyes made any difference
insights.locator: Australia's access disappeared alongside everyone else's

insights.claim: As recursive self-improvement and AGI loom, a single data centre could by early next decade be the equivalent of a country of geniuses, translating a decisive lead into an insurmountable one with gains multiplying across every dimension of state power.
insights.speaker: Tom Barber
insights.so_what: The winner-takes-all AGI scenario reframes compute concentration as existential geopolitical risk; concentration trajectory is the key variable to monitor.
insights.quote: a single data centre could be the equivalent of a country of geniuses
insights.locator: As recursive self-improvement looms over the horizon

insights.claim: An AGI hegemon would be more powerful than any country in history, locking in first-mover advantage, hoovering up the majority of global GDP and relegating everyone else to bystanders, with even a benevolent hegemon leaving everyone else's sovereignty conditional.
insights.speaker: Tom Barber
insights.so_what: If credible, the GDP-capture dynamic makes AGI leadership the ultimate macro asymmetry; even benign outcomes erode national sovereignty — a tail risk for all non-frontier economies.
insights.quote: Even a benevolent AGI hegemon would leave everyone else's sovereignty conditional.
insights.locator: An AGI hegemon would be far more powerful

insights.claim: Frontier labs are subject to the domestic legislation of the countries they sit in (a power-limiting mechanism for now), whereas the laws and norms between states are voluntary with no enforcement — so as long as the AI industry's centre of gravity stays in the US, that power compounds.
insights.speaker: Tom Barber
insights.so_what: Domestic regulation is the only real check on lab power, and it accrues entirely to the US; the geographic concentration of AI is itself the compounding risk.
insights.locator: The handful of labs at the frontier

insights.claim: AI depends on the physical world — supply chains, chip manufacturing, data centre build-outs, energy supply — each a bottleneck on frontier development, and Australia's mineral resources and vast renewable energy potential are clear assets for securing a foothold in the AI value chain.
insights.speaker: Tom Barber
insights.so_what: Minerals and energy are the tradeable chokepoints non-frontier nations can exploit; bullish strategic value for Australian critical-minerals and renewables as AI-value-chain leverage.
insights.quote: Translating agency into influence means exploiting these chokepoints to secure a foothold in the AI value chain
insights.locator: AI progress has been driven by relentless scaling

insights.claim: The logic of dilution is collective — the more countries that hold a stake, the harder it becomes for any single one to dominate — so Australia should push for a comprehensive global AI treaty including provisions on distributing AI's windfalls.
insights.speaker: Tom Barber
insights.so_what: Collective governance and windfall-distribution treaties are the proposed counterweight to concentration; watch for middle-power coalition-building on AI rules as a forward indicator.
insights.quote: The logic of dilution is collective
insights.locator: None of this should be done in isolation
